slide_003.appears_in: In Java, such a group of objects manipulated as a single entity is called a collection ◮ Java language environment (JDK) provides a Collections Framework that hold together several classes and interfaces of the type collection ◮ Quiz: are you able to differentiate between Java, JVM and JDK?

slide_004.appears_in: Collections vs ADTs ► There are familiar composite data structures behaving as collections called Abstract Data Types (ADT) Main among these are the List, Stack (LIFO), Queue (FIFO), Tree, and Table ►

Collections vs ADTs ► There are familiar composite data structures behaving as collections called Abstract Data Types (ADT) Main among these are the List, Stack (LIFO), Queue (FIFO), Tree, and Table ►

Each of these ADTs have unique interfaces (methods) that clearly identify the specific ADT through its behaviour While ADTs are useful, not having a common interface for manipulating these collections was a disadvantage ▶

Collections vs ADTs ► There are familiar composite data structures behaving as collections called Abstract Data Types (ADT) Main among these are the List, Stack (LIFO), Queue (FIFO), Tree, and Table ►

slide_005.appears_in: In the Java Collections Framework, there are 2 main root interface classes: 1.

Java pre- and post- Collections ◮ Object-oriented program designs can benefit from a framework of classes providing the functionality of a collection if they all perform similar tasks in a similar manner ◮ Originally, JDK provided the ability to group objects through Arrays (as a native language feature) and Vectors or Hashtables (as utility classes) ◮

slide_008.appears_in: ◮ Consistent API: The API has a basic set of interfaces (Collection, Set, List, Queue, Deque, Map, etc) and a set of classes that implement these interfaces (HashSet, TreeSet, ArrayList, LinkedList, ArrayDeque, HashMap, TreeMap, Vector, etc) ◮ All the implementation classes have some common set of methods ◮ Reduced programming effort: Due to the use of a common set of conceptually abstract methods, programmers can effectively use the OOP concepts of Abstraction and Encapsulation ◮ Increased program performance: Availability of high-performance implementations of essential data structures and algorithms improves both memory use and run-time efficiency

slide_009.appears_in: Each type of Java Collection has a different level of functionality and memory overhead ◮

slide_011.appears_in: The threshold at which rehashing happens along with capacity increase is computed as Capacity x Load Factor and has an initial default value of 12 ◮ Rehashing is the process of doubling the capacity of the HashMap after it reaches its threshold ◮ Quiz: what is the default sequence of rehashing sizes for HashMap?
The fraction of buckets used after which the capacity is increased is the load factor ◮

The threshold at which rehashing happens along with capacity increase is computed as Capacity x Load Factor and has an initial default value of 12 ◮ Rehashing is the process of doubling the capacity of the HashMap after it reaches its threshold ◮ Quiz: what is the default sequence of rehashing sizes for HashMap?
The number of storage buckets a HashMap can hold when it is instantiated is the initial capacity 2.

slide_012.appears_in: ◮ JVM implements HashMap as an array of HashMap\$Entry objects with each object having 4 fields: 1. int hash value computed using a hash function 2.

slide_018.appears_in: ◮ ArrayList class is an implementation of a dynamic array data structure where the size of the array automatically changes when items are dynamically added (or removed) ◮

slide_022.appears_in: ◮ Hash-based collections have a much larger overhead ◮ Additional data stored in hash-based collections improve the performance of search for items and therefore insertion and deletion ◮ Search time is constant for hash-based collections most of the time ◮ Search time is linear for array collections ◮ Carefully evaluate the memory footprint and run-time performance trade-off when deciding on a collection for data storage ◮ Collections expand by reallocation, which is a computationally expensive operation ◮ Collections nearly always does not automatically shrink when items are removed as it imposes a heavy performance penalty
